title: Third Level Graduate Programme (Stamp 1G)
content:
The Third Level Graduate Programme allows non-EEA graduates who have completed a recognised third-level qualification in Ireland to remain in Ireland and seek employment. Graduates are granted a Stamp 1G immigration permission.

Eligibility
To be eligible for the Third Level Graduate Programme, you must:
- Have completed a full-time course at a recognised Irish institution
- Hold a degree, master's or PhD (NFQ Level 7 or above) from an Irish higher education institution
- Have held Stamp 2 student permission while studying in Ireland
- Apply within 6 months of the date of your award (the date your college confirms your results)
- Your student permission (Stamp 2) must not have expired at the time you apply

Duration of Stamp 1G permission
- Graduates of a Level 8 (honours degree) programme: 12 months
- Graduates of a Level 9 (master's degree) or Level 10 (PhD) programme: 24 months

Work entitlements on Stamp 1G
While on Stamp 1G, you can:
- Work in any job without an employment permit
- Work full-time (no restrictions on hours)
- Be self-employed or set up a business

How to apply
Applications are made online through the Immigration Service Delivery (ISD) portal. You will need:
- Valid passport
- Proof of your qualification (official transcript or graduation letter)
- Evidence that you completed a full-time course
- Proof of private health insurance
- Evidence of sufficient funds to support yourself
- Application fee (€300)

Applications must be submitted online through the ISD portal. Processing times are typically 4-8 weeks.

Transitioning from Stamp 2
Once your Stamp 2 expires, you should apply for Stamp 1G before expiry if possible, or apply for renewal of your Stamp 2 if your results have not yet been confirmed. You cannot work full-time on Stamp 2.

After Stamp 1G
At the end of your Stamp 1G period, you may be eligible to apply for a different immigration permission depending on your employment situation, including a Critical Skills Employment Permit or a General Employment Permit if you secure employment in an eligible role.

title: Critical Skills Employment Permit
content:
The Critical Skills Employment Permit (CSEP) is designed to attract highly skilled workers in occupations where there are shortages in Ireland. It is a long-term permit that allows you to bring your family to Ireland and apply for longer-term residence.

Eligibility
To qualify for a Critical Skills Employment Permit, you must:
- Have a job offer from an Irish employer
- The job must be on the Critical Skills Occupations List OR have a minimum salary of €64,000 per year
- Jobs on the Critical Skills Occupations List must have a minimum salary of €38,000 per year
- Possess the relevant qualifications and skills for the role
- Your employer must be registered with Revenue and have a valid PRISM number

Critical Skills Occupations List
The Critical Skills Occupations List includes roles in:
- ICT (software developers, data scientists, cybersecurity)
- Healthcare (doctors, nurses, physiotherapists)
- Engineering
- Finance and accounting
- Construction (certain senior roles)
- Research and development

The list is reviewed regularly by the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment.

Processing times
The DETE aims to process straightforward applications within 1-3 months. However, complex applications may take longer. You can check current processing times on the ISD website.

Duration and renewal
A Critical Skills Employment Permit is issued for 2 years initially. It can be renewed for a further 3 years. After 5 years, you may be eligible to apply for a Long-Term Residence Permit or naturalisation as an Irish citizen.

Family reunification
A Critical Skills Employment Permit holder can apply for family members to join them in Ireland. Your spouse or partner can apply for a Stamp 1G permission which allows them to work in any employment without restriction.

Changing employer on CSEP
You can change employer on a Critical Skills Employment Permit after you have been in employment with the original employer for 12 months. If you change employer within 12 months, you must apply for a new permit from your new employer.

title: Universal Social Charge (USC)
content:
The Universal Social Charge (USC) is a tax you pay on your gross income. It replaced the income levy and health contribution in January 2011. USC applies to almost all income, including income from a trade, profession, salary, benefit-in-kind, and rental income.

Who pays USC
You must pay USC if your gross income is over €13,000 in a year. If your total income for the year is €13,000 or less, you are exempt from USC.

Medical card holders aged under 70 and people aged 70 or over pay USC at a reduced rate (maximum 2%).

USC rates for 2025
- 0.5% on income up to €12,012
- 2% on income between €12,013 and €25,760
- 3% on income between €25,761 and €70,044
- 8% on income over €70,044

Self-employed income over €100,000 is subject to an additional 3% USC surcharge.

USC and PAYE
For most employees, USC is collected through the PAYE (Pay As You Earn) system. Your employer deducts USC from your pay and sends it to Revenue. Your payslip should show your gross pay, USC deducted, PAYE income tax deducted, and PRSI deducted.

Income exempt from USC
- Social welfare payments
- Occupational pensions that qualified for tax relief when they were being made
- Income already subject to DIRT (Deposit Interest Retention Tax)
- Certain salary sacrifice arrangements

Filing and paying
If you are a PAYE employee with no other income, your employer handles USC payment. If you have other income (rental income, self-employment), you must file a Form 11 tax return by October 31 each year (or by November 14 if using Revenue's Online Service, ROS).

title: Jobseeker's Benefit
content:
Jobseeker's Benefit is a weekly payment from the Department of Social Protection to people who are unemployed and covered by social insurance (PRSI).

Eligibility conditions
To qualify for Jobseeker's Benefit, you must:
- Be aged between 18 and 66 years
- Be fully unemployed or work less than 4 days per week
- Be available for and genuinely seeking full-time work
- Have enough PRSI contributions — at least 104 contributions paid since you first started work, and at least 39 contributions paid or credited in the governing contribution year (currently 2023 for 2025 claims)
- Be habitually resident in Ireland

PRSI contributions
Jobseeker's Benefit is based on Pay Related Social Insurance (PRSI) contributions. PRSI Class A applies to most employees and is the relevant class for Jobseeker's Benefit. You need 104 PRSI contributions paid (not credited) over your working life, AND 39 paid or credited contributions in the relevant governing year.

How much you get
The maximum personal rate of Jobseeker's Benefit in 2025 is €244 per week. You may receive additional amounts for a qualified adult and qualified children.

The rate you receive depends on your earnings in the relevant tax year. People who earned more may receive a higher rate (up to the maximum).

How long it lasts
- If you have 260 or more paid contributions: you can receive Jobseeker's Benefit for 9 months (234 days)
- If you have fewer than 260 paid contributions: you can receive it for 6 months (156 days)

After Jobseeker's Benefit ends, you may be eligible for Jobseeker's Allowance (a means-tested payment) if you have insufficient means.

Difference from Jobseeker's Allowance
Jobseeker's Benefit is based on PRSI contributions — it is not means-tested. Jobseeker's Allowance is means-tested (your income and savings are taken into account) and does not require PRSI contributions, but you must meet a habitual residence condition and a means test.

How to apply
You should apply for Jobseeker's Benefit as soon as you become unemployed. You can apply online at mywelfare.ie. Bring your PPS number, proof of identity, and details of your last employer.

title: Jobseeker's Allowance
content:
Jobseeker's Allowance is a means-tested payment from the Department of Social Protection (DSP) for people who are unemployed. Unlike Jobseeker's Benefit, it does not require PRSI contributions. However, it is means-tested, meaning your income and savings will affect the amount you receive.

Eligibility conditions
To qualify for Jobseeker's Allowance, you must:
- Be aged 18 or over (some conditions apply for those under 25)
- Be fully unemployed or work less than 4 days per week
- Be available for and genuinely seeking full-time employment
- Pass a means test
- Meet the habitual residence condition (you must have been living in Ireland and intend to stay)

Means test
The means test assesses your weekly income from all sources. This includes:
- Cash income (earnings from employment, self-employment, rental income)
- Value of capital (savings, investments, property other than your home)
- Spouse or civil partner's income

Means-disregards: The first €20 per day of casual earnings (up to 3 days) is disregarded.

Rates in 2025
The maximum personal rate for those aged 26 and over is €244 per week. Reduced rates apply to those aged 18-24:
- Aged 18-24: €141.70 per week
- Aged 25: €181.70 per week
- Aged 26 and over: €244 per week

Working while claiming Jobseeker's Allowance
You can work part-time (up to 3 days per week) while claiming Jobseeker's Allowance. Your means are assessed based on your part-time earnings. A daily means disregard of €20 applies.

How to apply
You can apply online at mywelfare.ie or at your local Intreo Centre or Social Welfare Branch Office. You will need your PPSN and details of your income and circumstances.

title: How your income tax is calculated in Ireland
content:
Income tax in Ireland is calculated based on your total income from all sources, minus certain tax credits and reliefs. Most employees pay tax through the PAYE (Pay As You Earn) system, where their employer deducts tax automatically.

Income tax rates for 2025
Ireland has two income tax rates:
- 20% (the standard rate) on income up to your standard rate cut-off point
- 40% (the higher rate) on income above your standard rate cut-off point

Standard rate cut-off points for 2025:
- Single person: €44,000
- Married couple/civil partnership (one income): €53,000
- Married couple/civil partnership (two incomes): up to €88,000
- One-parent family: €48,000

Tax credits
Tax credits reduce the amount of tax you pay. Everyone gets a Personal Tax Credit of €1,875. Employees get an Employee Tax Credit (PAYE credit) of €1,875. A single employee therefore has credits totalling €3,750.

Other common tax credits:
- Home Carer Tax Credit: €1,800
- Single Person Child Carer Credit: €1,750
- Dependent Relative Tax Credit: €245
- Incapacitated Child Tax Credit: €3,300
- Age Tax Credit (65 or over): €245

Exemption limits
For 2025, if your income is below €18,000 (single) or €36,000 (married couple) you may be exempt from income tax, provided you are aged 65 or over. For people under 65, there is no income tax exemption limit — any income over your tax credits is taxable.

Universal Social Charge (USC)
In addition to income tax, most people pay USC on their gross income. See the USC information page for rates and details.

Pay Related Social Insurance (PRSI)
Most employees also pay PRSI, which funds social welfare benefits including Jobseeker's Benefit, State Pension, and Maternity Benefit. The standard employee rate is 4.1% on all income for those aged under 66.

title: Medical Card
content:
A medical card allows you to access a range of health services free of charge. These services include visits to a GP, prescribed medications, public hospital services, dental, optical and aural services.

Eligibility
Medical cards are means-tested. Your eligibility depends on your weekly income. You must be ordinarily resident in Ireland to apply.

Income guidelines for medical card (2025)
- Single person aged under 70 living alone: €184 per week
- Single person aged under 70 not living alone: €164 per week
- Couple or lone parent (aged under 70): €266.50 per week
- Allowances are made for dependent children and certain expenses

Hardship provision
Even if your income is above the guidelines, a medical card may be granted if your medical expenses would cause undue hardship.

What the medical card covers
- GP visits (free of charge)
- Prescribed medications (subject to the prescription charge of €1.50 per item, max €15 per month per person or €25 per family per month)
- Hospital outpatient and inpatient services
- Dental examinations and some treatments
- Eye tests and glasses
- Hearing tests and hearing aids
- Mental health services
- Maternity and infant care schemes

How to apply
You can apply online at medicalcard.ie. You will need your PPSN, details of your income (payslips or Revenue assessment), and details of any medical expenses.

GP Visit Card
If your income is above the medical card threshold, you may qualify for a GP Visit Card, which covers GP visits only (not other services). The income thresholds for GP Visit Cards are approximately twice those for medical cards.

title: Personal Public Service Number (PPSN)
content:
A Personal Public Service Number (PPSN) is a unique identifier used by the Irish State to register you for public services. You need a PPSN to access most government services, pay taxes, receive social welfare payments, apply for a driving licence, and register with a GP.

Who needs a PPSN
You need a PPSN if you are:
- Working in Ireland and paying tax
- Claiming a social welfare benefit or payment
- Accessing public health services
- Registering a child for state services
- Involved in property transactions

How to apply for a PPSN
You must apply in person at your local Intreo Centre or Social Welfare Branch Office. You cannot apply by post or online for a new PPSN. To find your nearest office, visit gov.ie.

Documents required
- Proof of identity: valid passport, national ID card, or Garda National Immigration Bureau (GNIB) card
- Proof of address: utility bill, bank statement, or official letter dated within 3 months
- Evidence of why you need a PPSN: letter from employer, letter from a Government Department, or other relevant documentation

For non-EEA nationals, your Irish Residence Permit (IRP) serves as your proof of immigration status.

Processing time
Once you attend your appointment with all required documents, your PPSN is usually issued within a few weeks. You will receive a letter with your PPSN.

Finding your PPSN
If you already have a PPSN and cannot remember it, you can find it on:
- Your payslip
- Correspondence from Revenue or the Department of Social Protection
- Your public services card (if you have one)
- Revenue's myAccount service

Children born in Ireland are assigned a PPSN automatically.

title: National Minimum Wage
content:
The National Minimum Wage is the minimum hourly rate of pay that employers must pay their employees in Ireland.

Current rates (2025)
- Standard national minimum wage: €13.50 per hour
- Applies to employees aged 20 and over

Younger worker rates:
- Aged under 20: Sub-minimum rates apply. The Government has announced a phased move to one universal rate.
- In practice as of 2025, the minimum rate for those under 20 is a percentage of the adult rate, but most employers pay the full rate

Who it applies to
The National Minimum Wage applies to most employees. There are very limited exceptions:
- Close relatives of the employer working in the family home or farm
- People doing approved apprenticeships (who have structured wage progressions)

Trainee rates
Employee training or structured courses that are approved may have different rates.

Inclusion in pay calculation
The following count toward minimum wage:
- Basic pay
- Shift premium
- Unsocial hours payment
- Piece rates

The following do not count:
- Overtime premium
- Call-out premium
- Tips and gratuities
- On-call payments
- Allowances not related to work performed

Statutory Sick Pay
From 2025, employers must provide a minimum of 5 days of statutory sick pay per year at 70% of an employee's normal daily wage, subject to a daily maximum. This is separate from the minimum wage.

Enforcement
The Workplace Relations Commission (WRC) enforces minimum wage legislation. Employees who believe they are being paid below the minimum wage can make a complaint to the WRC. Adjudicators can order back-pay and other redress.

title: PAYE Tax Credit
content:
The PAYE (Pay As You Earn) tax credit — also called the Employee Tax Credit — is a tax credit available to people who pay income tax through the PAYE system.

Amount
The PAYE tax credit for 2025 is €1,875. This reduces the amount of income tax you pay.

How it works
The PAYE tax credit is subtracted from your total tax liability after your income has been taxed at the standard and higher rates.

Example: A single employee earning €40,000:
- First €44,000 taxed at 20% = €8,000
- Less Personal Tax Credit (€1,875) = €6,125
- Less PAYE Tax Credit (€1,875) = €4,250
- Total income tax = €4,250

Note: USC and PRSI are calculated separately.

Claiming the PAYE credit
The PAYE credit is automatically applied to employees through the PAYE system. You do not need to claim it separately. Your employer applies it via your Tax Credit Certificate.

If you are not claiming the full PAYE credit (for example, you work part-time or start work mid-year), you may be able to claim a refund through Revenue's myAccount service.

PAYE credit and self-employment
The PAYE credit is not available to self-employed individuals. Self-employed people can claim an Earned Income Tax Credit instead, currently set at €1,875 — the same value as the PAYE credit.

title: Child Benefit
content:
Child Benefit is a monthly payment to the parents or guardians of children under 16 years of age, or under 18 if the child is in full-time education, has a disability, or has a medical condition that makes it impossible to support themselves.

Amount
Child Benefit in 2025 is €140 per month for each child.

Multiple children bonus:
For twins, the rate is €210 per month per child (150% of the standard rate).
For triplets or more, the rate is €280 per month per child (200% of the standard rate).

Eligibility
You qualify for Child Benefit if:
- Your child is under 16 (or under 18 if in full-time education or has a qualifying disability)
- You are the child's parent or primary carer
- The child normally lives with you
- You and the child are habitually resident in Ireland

Child Benefit is not means-tested — it is paid regardless of income.

When it stops
Child Benefit normally stops when your child turns 16. It can continue until your child turns 18 if they:
- Are in full-time education
- Have a disability or illness that prevents them from supporting themselves

How to apply
For a child born in Ireland, Child Benefit is registered automatically through the civil registration of the birth. You should receive notification from the DSP.

For children born outside Ireland, or if you move to Ireland with children, you must apply using Form CB1 from your local DSP office or Intreo Centre.

Other child payments
In addition to Child Benefit, other supports may be available:
- Working Family Payment (WFP): for families in low-paid employment
- Back to School Clothing and Footwear Allowance
- School Meals Scheme
- One-Parent Family Payment

title: Registration of Non-EEA Nationals — Irish Residence Permit (IRP)
content:
Most non-EEA nationals who come to live in Ireland for more than 90 days must register with the Irish immigration authorities. Upon registration, you receive an Irish Residence Permit (IRP) — a card that shows your immigration permission, Stamp type, and expiry date.

Who must register
You must register if you are a non-EEA national who:
- Has permission to remain in Ireland for more than 90 days
- Is in Ireland for work (with an employment permit), study (Stamp 2), or other long-term purposes

Where to register
In Dublin: Register at the Burgh Quay Registration Office (by appointment only). Appointments must be booked in advance on the ISD website.

Outside Dublin: Register at your local Garda Síochána immigration registration office.

Documents needed for registration
- Valid passport
- Letter or notification of your immigration permission (from ISD or your employer/college)
- Employment permit or other relevant permit
- Proof of address in Ireland
- Registration fee (€300) — payable by card at Burgh Quay

Children under 18 are exempt from the registration fee.

Irish Residence Permit (IRP)
After registering, you receive an IRP card (previously called the GNIB card). The card shows:
- Your name
- Date of birth
- Nationality
- Stamp type (e.g., Stamp 2, Stamp 1G, Stamp 4)
- Expiry date of your permission

Renewing your IRP
You must renew your IRP before it expires. You should register renewal at least 4-6 weeks before the expiry date. Failure to renew on time can create complications for future immigration applications.

Stamp types
- Stamp 2: Student permission — limited work rights (20 hours/week during term)
- Stamp 1G: Graduate permission — full work rights for 12 or 24 months
- Stamp 4: Long-term residence or family reunification — full work rights, broad access to public services
- Stamp 1: Employment permit holder — must work for specific employer on permit

title: Renting in Ireland — Tenants' Rights
content:
If you are renting a home in Ireland, you have rights and responsibilities under the Residential Tenancies Acts. Your landlord also has rights and responsibilities.

Types of rental tenancy
1. Fixed-term tenancy: You agree to rent for a set period (e.g., 12 months). Neither party can end the tenancy without good reason during this period.
2. Part IV tenancy: After 6 months of renting, you automatically acquire security of tenure under Part IV of the Residential Tenancies Act. This means you can remain in the property for up to 6 years (in most circumstances).

Security of tenure
After 6 months of continuous occupation, you become a Part IV tenant. Your landlord can only end your tenancy for specific reasons, including:
- The landlord needs the property for their own use or for a family member
- The landlord intends to sell the property
- The property needs substantial refurbishment
- You have breached your tenancy obligations (e.g., non-payment of rent)

Notice periods
For tenancies of 6 months to 1 year, the landlord must give at least 90 days notice. For longer tenancies, longer notice periods apply (up to 224 days for tenancies of 8 years or more).

Rent Pressure Zones (RPZs)
Most urban areas in Ireland are Rent Pressure Zones. In RPZs, rent increases are limited to 2% per year or the rate of inflation (HICP), whichever is lower. Your landlord cannot increase rent more frequently than every 12 months.

Deposit
By law, your deposit cannot exceed more than one month's rent. The Residential Tenancies Board (RTB) maintains the Deposit Protection Scheme to hold and return deposits fairly.

Residential Tenancies Board (RTB)
The RTB is the state agency that manages and resolves disputes between landlords and tenants. If you have a dispute with your landlord about rent, maintenance, deposit return, or notice, you can make a complaint to the RTB.

title: Overview of Employment Permits in Ireland
content:
If you are a non-EEA national and want to work in Ireland, you will generally need an employment permit, unless you are exempt (for example, if you hold a Stamp 4 or Stamp 1G permission).

Types of employment permits
There are nine types of employment permit in Ireland:
1. Critical Skills Employment Permit: For highly skilled workers in occupations on the Critical Skills list, or earning over €64,000 per year
2. General Employment Permit: For most other occupations not on the Ineligible Occupations List, with a minimum salary of €34,000 per year (certain exceptions)
3. Intra-Company Transfer Employment Permit: For employees being transferred from an overseas branch to an Irish branch of the same company
4. Reactivation Employment Permit: For people who held an employment permit that has expired or lapsed and who are in an irregular situation
5. Contract for Services Employment Permit: For non-EEA contractors providing services in Ireland
6. Sport and Cultural Employment Permit
7. Exchange Agreement Employment Permit
8. Internship Employment Permit
9. Researcher Employment Permit

Who can apply
For most permit types, either the employer or the employee can make the application. However, the employer is responsible for ensuring their employee has the right to work.

Labour Market Needs Test
For the General Employment Permit, employers must generally conduct a Labour Market Needs Test before hiring a non-EEA national. This means advertising the position to EEA nationals first. Certain roles are exempt from the test.

Critical Skills Occupations List
The Critical Skills Occupations List is reviewed periodically by the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment. Occupations on the list include many ICT roles, healthcare professionals, engineers, and scientists.

Ineligible Occupations List
Some occupations are on the Ineligible Occupations List and cannot be filled using a General Employment Permit. These are typically low-skilled or low-paid roles.

Employee protections
Employment permit holders are fully protected by Irish employment law. Your employer cannot use your immigration status to exploit or coerce you. If you are being exploited, contact the Workplace Relations Commission or An Garda Síochána.

title: Opening a Bank Account in Ireland
content:
You do not need to be an Irish citizen to open a bank account in Ireland. However, banks have different requirements for proof of identity and address.

Types of bank account
- Current account: For day-to-day banking (direct debits, card payments, salary payments)
- Savings account: For saving money, usually with higher interest rates
- Basic bank account: A no-frills current account that banks must offer if you do not qualify for a standard current account

Main banks in Ireland
- AIB (Allied Irish Banks)
- Bank of Ireland
- Permanent TSB
- An Post (via PostPoint and AnPost Money)
- Credit Unions (community-based, member-owned)
- N26, Revolut (digital-only banks, easier to open without Irish address proof)

Documents typically required
- Proof of identity: Valid passport or national ID card
- Proof of address: Utility bill, bank statement, or official correspondence dated within 3-6 months. For people who have just arrived, an employer letter, tenancy agreement, or letter from a government department may be accepted
- Some banks may require additional documentation for non-EEA nationals

Difficulty opening an account
Recently arrived non-EEA nationals can find it difficult to open a bank account because they lack an Irish address history. Options include:
- Digital banks (N26, Revolut) which often have lighter requirements
- Credit unions
- An Post's current account service
- Basic bank accounts (banks must offer these under EU regulations)

Credit Unions
Credit unions are cooperative financial institutions that offer current accounts, savings accounts, and loans to members. Membership is usually based on a common bond (living in an area, working for an employer, etc.). Many credit unions are welcoming to new arrivals and may have more flexible account-opening requirements.
